name: Eastern Saudi (Sharqiyya) Arabic

ancestors.notes: The Eastern Province (al-Sharqiyya) of Saudi Arabia — al-Hasa/al-Ahsāʾ and al-Qaṭīf — is dialectologically part of the Gulf (khalījī) area, not the Najdi or Hejazi ones.

notes: Eastern Saudi / Sharqiyya Arabic (شرقاوي), the Arabic of the Eastern Province of Saudi Arabia: al-Hasa (al-Ahsāʾ, Hofuf) and al-Qaṭīf. It is a GULF (khalījī) dialect, patterning with Bahrain and the lower Gulf rather than with Najd or the Hejaz, so it parents to ar-x-gulf and inherits the Gulf reflexes: qaf ق → [ɡ] (with /q/ robustly retained in a large stratum of local and learned lexemes — the /q/~[ɡ] alternation is the central topic of Al-Taisan 2022 on Hasawi; Smeaton 1973; Prochazka 1988), kaf ك → [tʃ] affrication (allophone_rules GULF_K_AFFRICATION, inherited; Al-Taisan 2022; Aljumah 2008), interdentals ث /θ/ ذ /ð/ retained, and the Gulf /ɡ/ → [dʒ] affrication of the qaf reflex. DEFINING DELTA modeled here: jīm ج → [dʒ] (affricate) as the primary reflex. This is the sedentary Eastern reflex on the direct evidence of the leaf's own primary source: Al-Taisan 2022 documents Hasawi jīm as /ʤ/ throughout — in the consonant inventory (p.12) and in every Hasawi example — and cites the *ǧīm→[j] glide-lenition only as a feature of 'some Northern/Eastern Arabian dialects' (Johnstone 1967), never as an attested Hasawi reflex (pp.2, 155). The Baḥārna (sedentary Shiʿi) community that dominates al-Qaṭīf and is strong in al-Hasa likewise carries the affricate [dʒ], not [j]: in the Gulf sect isogloss [j] is the marked reflex of the SUNNI ʕArab dialect (prestige, royal-family/media Bahraini) while [dʒ] is the marked reflex of the ŠIʿi Baḥārna dialect (Holes 1980/2016, reported in Alaodini 2019 p.94). The earlier version of this leaf had this attribution INVERTED — it modeled [j] as a 'Baḥārna Shiʿi' reflex, which the sources contradict. The glide [j] is kept as the SECONDARY variant: it is the reflex of the Sunni ʕArab townsmen and of the Dawāsir tribe (Sunni, Najdi-origin) who acquired [j] under Bahraini prestige contact and are now receding back to [dʒ] (Alaodini 2019: [dʒ] 76%, [j] traditional/receding). For the Dawāsir-descended Dammam urban variety — Najdi-Bedouin core (retained interdentals, gahawa syndrome, *dˤ→ðˤ merger, Najdi short-vowel rules, [ɒː] rounding) with a Gulf overlay and the traditional [j] — see the sibling stub ar-SA-x-dawasir. The Eastern Province is genuinely split (sedentary Baḥārna/Hasawi Gulf type vs Dawāsir/ʕujmān Najdi-Bedouin type); this leaf models the sedentary Gulf majority. NO ar-SA umbrella node: this leaf demonstrates why — Eastern Saudi is Gulf, southwestern Saudi is peninsular-archaic, Najd is Najdi and the Hejaz is Hejazi; there is no pan-Saudi phonology, so ar-SA stays unassigned (Prochazka 1988 surveys the country precisely as a patchwork). INPUT CONTRACT: transcribes orthography+harakat; short vowels surface only when the input is vocalised.

sources.author: Al-Taisan, H.
sources.title: Velar, uvular and pharyngeal alternations in Hasawi Arabic: a Harmonic Serialism OT account (PhD thesis)
sources.notes: Open-access. On Hasawi Arabic (al-Hasa, Eastern Province). Consonant inventory (Table 2-1, p.12) has both /q/ and /ɡ/ phonemic and the affricates /tʃ, dʒ/; qaf → [ɡ] with /q/ retained in a large NON-religious lexical stratum (p.13, p.79; Smeaton 1973; Prochazka 1988; Mstafawi 2006). Jīm is /ʤ/ (affricate) throughout the inventory and every Hasawi example (e.g. [dʒa] 'he came' p.20; [firiːʤ] 'team' p.166); the /dʒ/→[j] glide-lenition is only CITED for 'some Northern/Eastern Arabian dialects' (Johnstone 1967, pp.2, 155), never attested in Hasawi — so this leaf's primary jīm reflex is [dʒ]. The /ɡ/ (qaf reflex) affricates to [dʒ]/[dz] before front /i/ (Example 4-3b, p.81; p.166), and /k/→[tʃ]/[ts] before front vowels (p.81, out of the thesis's scope but noted). Interdentals retained and /dˤ/→/ðˤ/ merger (p.13); /ʒ/ not attested (pp.13, 132). The thesis's core is the /q/~/ʁ/ uvular alternation, which is lexically conditioned and left unmodelled here.

sources.author: Holes, C.
sources.title: Modern Arabic: Structures, Functions, and Varieties
sources.notes: Bahrani (Baḥārna) Arabic — the sedentary Shiʿi Gulf type of al-Qaṭīf and Bahrain. On the *ǧīm reflex the sect isogloss runs OPPOSITE to what an earlier version of this leaf assumed: [j] is the marked reflex of the SUNNI ʕArab dialect (locally prestigious, used by the Bahraini royal family and in Gulf media), while [dʒ] is the marked reflex of the ŠIʿi Baḥārna dialect (Holes 1980, 1983, 2016; reported with page pins in Alaodini 2019 p.94). Both communities have [ɡ] for *qāf. Basis for modeling ج → [dʒ] as the sedentary Eastern Saudi majority reflex (Baḥārna + Hasawi) with [j] as the Sunni-ʕArab/Dawāsir secondary variant.

sources.author: Johnstone, T.M.
sources.title: Eastern Arabian Dialect Studies
sources.notes: The foundational survey of the Gulf/Eastern Arabian dialects, including the *ǧīm → [j] lenition and *k → [tʃ] / *q-reflex → [dʒ] affrication that define the area.

sources.author: Prochazka, T.
sources.title: Saudi Arabian Dialects
sources.publisher: Kegan Paul International (Library of Arabic Linguistics, Monograph 8)
sources.notes: The first country-wide survey of Saudi dialects, north to south. Documents the Eastern Province (Hofuf/al-Hasa) as a Gulf-type area with qaf → [ɡ], and the country as a dialect patchwork with no single 'Saudi' phonology — grounds the decision to leave ar-SA unassigned.

sources.author: Alaodini, H.
sources.title: A Sociolinguistic Study of the Dawāsir Dialect in Dammam, Eastern Arabia: Variation and Change in a Tribal Community (PhD thesis)
sources.notes: Sociolinguistic study of the Dawāsir (Sunni, Najdi-origin tribe) in urban Dammam. Establishes the Eastern Province as previously-understudied outside al-Hasa and dialectally split. Key facts corroborating this leaf: the Gulf *ǧīm sect isogloss — [j] is the SUNNI ʕArab prestige reflex, [dʒ] the ŠIʿi Baḥārna reflex (p.94, citing Holes) — and the Dawāsir [j] is a Bahraini-contact acquisition now receding to [dʒ] (76% [dʒ], p.125). The Dawāsir carry a Najdi-Bedouin layer (retained interdentals + *dˤ→ðˤ merger p.42; gahawa syndrome p.46; *k→[tʃ] kaškaša and *ɡ→[dʒ] affrication p.43-45; no [ts]/[dz]; Najdi short-vowel rules, no labial i>u, unlike Bahrani p.54; word-medial /ɑː/→[ɒː] rounding p.52-83, a pan-Eastern feature shared with Hasa/Bahrain). Basis for the ar-SA-x-dawasir sibling stub.

location.notes: Al-Hofuf, al-Hasa, Eastern Province, Saudi Arabia.

stress.notes: Quantity-sensitive Arabic stress, inherited from the Gulf parent; see ar-SA-x-najd for the cascade and input contract. Prosodic clitics: monosyllabic function words (negator maː, prepositions fiː/min/ʕan/ʕa/li/bi, vocative jaː, copula-negator muː/miʃ, conditional law/loː, wa-proclitic combinations) attach to a host and fall inside the host's stress domain, so they carry no word stress (Watson 2002, The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic, OUP, ch.3). Pan-Arabic class; the per-lect form list reflects the function words attested in this lect. Orthographic test — a clitic homograph (e.g. verb yā 'came') is destressed in every occurrence.
